summary:
The Mughal-Sikh Conflict (c. 1606–1716 AD) transformed the Sikh community from a primarily devotional and pacifist movement into a militarized nation capable of resisting Mughal imperial power — a transformation driven by successive Mughal persecutions of Sikh Gurus and the resulting development of the martial identity embodied in the Khalsa (the order of initiated Sikhs). The conflict began when Emperor Jahangir ordered the execution of Guru Arjan Dev Ji (1606), the fifth Sikh Guru, after perceiving the growing Sikh community as a political threat. The sixth Guru, Hargobind, responded by transforming the Sikh movement: he carried two swords symbolizing spiritual and temporal authority (miri and piri), established an army, and built the Akal Takht (Throne of the Timeless) opposite the [[event:3507|Golden Temple]] — formalizing Sikhism's dual role as spiritual and political community.

The conflict intensified under [[event:2507|Aurangzeb]], who ordered the execution of the ninth Guru, Tegh Bahadur Ji (1675), reportedly for refusing to convert to Islam. This martyrdom galvanized the Sikh community: the tenth and final Guru, Gobind Singh, transformed the Sikh movement at Anandpur in 1699, creating the Khalsa — an initiated community of soldier-saints with distinctive symbols (the 'Five Ks') and a martial code requiring resistance to tyranny. After the Mughal execution of Gobind Singh's sons and decades of guerrilla warfare, the Sikhs under Banda Singh Bahadur briefly established control over the Punjab (1709–1716) before Mughal suppression — the first expression of Sikh political sovereignty that would eventually lead to the [[event:2369|Sikh Empire]].
